name: Barbell Back Squat
description: Builds full-leg strength and powerful hips.

setup: Stand balanced with feet about hip to shoulder width.
Brace your core and keep your chest tall.
Hold the barbell securely with weight centered over mid-foot.
movement: Sit your hips down and back as your knees track over your toes.
Lower until your thighs are about parallel or a comfortable depth.
Drive through your whole foot to stand tall and squeeze your glutes.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your quads doing most of the work, with your hamstrings, core helping you stay stable and upright.

commonFixes.fix: Press your knees out gently and keep your feet rooted. Think: spread the floor, and stay balanced.
Lift your chest and keep your upper back tight. Slow the descent until you can keep posture.

makeItEasier.name: Goblet Squat
makeItEasier.description: Hold a light dumbbell at your chest to practice depth and posture.

levelUp.description: Add a slower 3-second lower and pause at the bottom for extra strength and stability.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower into the squat, breathe out as you drive up to stand.

name: Tempo Back Squat
description: Slow lowering to build control and stability.

setup: Set up as you would for a standard back squat.
Brace your core and keep your chest tall.
Choose a load you can control for the full tempo.
movement: Lower for a slow 3-second count, staying balanced.
Pause briefly at the bottom if needed.
Drive up smoothly to standing.
Reset without rushing into the next rep.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel more control and stability through the whole squat, especially on the way down.

commonFixes.fix: Count out a full 3 seconds and keep tension throughout.
Stay over mid-foot and keep your chest lifted.

makeItEasier.name: Barbell Back Squat
makeItEasier.description: Use a normal tempo if the slow lowering is too challenging.

levelUp.description: Add a longer pause at the bottom for more strength off the hole.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before the descent, breathe out as you stand. Keep breathing steady.

name: Pause Back Squat
description: Pause at the bottom to build strength out of the hole.

setup: Set up as you would for a standard back squat.
Brace your core and keep your chest tall.
Choose a load you can control with a pause.
movement: Lower under control to your normal squat depth.
Pause for 1–2 seconds while staying tight.
Drive up smoothly to standing.
Reset without bouncing out of the bottom.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel strong quad and core engagement at the bottom without losing position.

commonFixes.fix: Stay braced and keep tension through your midsection.
Hold the pause still, then drive up smoothly.

makeItEasier.name: Barbell Back Squat
makeItEasier.description: Use a normal tempo if the pause breaks your position.

levelUp.description: Increase load gradually or extend the pause slightly.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before the descent, hold tension through the pause, breathe out as you stand.

name: Pin Squat
description: Dead-stop squat to build strength at sticking points.

setup: Set the safety pins at your target squat depth.
Unrack the bar and step back with feet shoulder width.
Brace your core and keep your chest tall.
movement: Lower under control until the bar touches the pins.
Pause briefly and stay tight without relaxing.
Drive up from the pins to standing.
Reset at the top before the next rep.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a powerful drive out of the bottom with strong quad and core engagement.

commonFixes.fix: Stay braced and keep your torso rigid before driving up.

commonFixes.issue: Pins set too low or too high
commonFixes.fix: Choose a depth that targets your sticking point with safe form.

makeItEasier.name: Pause Back Squat
makeItEasier.description: Use a paused squat without pins to build control at depth.

levelUp.description: Add load gradually or increase range by lowering pin height.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before the descent, hold tension on the pins, breathe out as you stand.

name: Box Squat
description: Squat to a box for depth control.
siblingReason: Removes depth anxiety and teaches the hip-back movement pattern. Good for beginners afraid of 'falling' in the squat, and for those with knee concerns as it limits forward knee travel.

setup: Set a box at a height that allows safe depth.
Unrack the bar and stand with feet about shoulder width.
Brace your core and keep your chest tall.
movement: Sit your hips back and down to the box under control.
Lightly touch the box without collapsing.
Drive through your feet to stand tall.
Reset at the top without locking out hard.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel quads and glutes working with a stable, controlled depth.

commonFixes.fix: Touch the box lightly and keep tension throughout the rep.
Press knees out gently and keep feet rooted.

makeItEasier.name: Bodyweight Squat
makeItEasier.description: Use bodyweight only to learn the pattern and depth.

levelUp.description: Lower the box height slightly or move to a full back squat without a box.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you stand. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Chair Sit-to-Stand
description: Beginner-friendly squat to a chair.

setup: Stand in front of a chair with feet hip-width apart.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
Reach your hips back toward the chair.
movement: Sit down with control, lightly touching the chair.
Drive through your feet to stand tall.
Keep your knees tracking over toes.
Repeat without dropping quickly.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel quads and glutes working with steady balance.

commonFixes.fix: Press knees out gently and keep feet rooted.
Slow down and control the descent.

levelUp.description: Progress to a box squat or bodyweight squat as control improves.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you sit, breathe out as you stand. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Safety Bar Squat
description: More upright squat with shoulder-friendly setup.
siblingReason: Reduces shoulder and wrist mobility demands of the back squat. Good for those with shoulder impingement, limited thoracic mobility, or post-shoulder surgery. Slightly shifts load forward compared to back squat.

setup: Set the safety bar on your upper back and grip the handles.
Stand tall with feet about shoulder width and brace your core.
Keep your chest lifted and elbows tucked.
movement: Sit your hips down and back as your knees track over toes.
Lower until thighs are about parallel or a comfortable depth.
Drive through your whole foot to stand tall.
Reset at the top without locking out hard.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel quads and glutes working with a more upright torso.

commonFixes.fix: Keep ribs down and drive your chest up as you stand.
Press knees out gently and keep feet rooted.

makeItEasier.name: Goblet Squat
makeItEasier.description: Use a dumbbell to learn depth and control with less load.

levelUp.description: Move to a barbell back squat if shoulder mobility allows.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you stand. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Barbell Front Squat
description: Quad-dominant squat that strengthens posture.
siblingReason: More upright torso than back squat reduces lower back stress and increases quad emphasis. Preferred for Olympic lifting carry-over and those with lower back sensitivity. Requires greater wrist/shoulder mobility in rack position.

setup: Take the bar in a front rack — either a clean grip — fingers under the bar with elbows high — or crossed arms with the bar on your front delts.
Keep your elbows high and in front of your body so the bar stays secure and your torso stays upright.
Stand with feet about hip to shoulder width, core braced, and the weight over your mid-foot.
movement: Sit your hips down and back as your knees track over your toes, but keep your torso more upright than in a back squat.
Keep your elbows high throughout so the bar doesn't roll forward; lower to parallel or a comfortable depth.
Drive through your whole foot to stand and squeeze your glutes at the top.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your quads doing most of the work, with your glutes, core, upper-back helping you stay stable and upright.

commonFixes.fix: Press your knees out gently and keep your feet rooted. Think: spread the floor, and stay balanced.
Lift your chest and keep your upper back tight. Slow the descent until you can keep posture.

makeItEasier.name: Goblet Squat
makeItEasier.description: Hold a light dumbbell at your chest to practice depth and posture.

levelUp.description: Add a slower 3-second lower and pause at the bottom for extra strength and stability.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you sit back into the squat, breathe out as you stand.

name: Goblet Squat
description: A beginner-friendly squat with great control.
siblingReason: Counterbalance from the dumbbell actively helps achieve depth and upright posture. Best first loaded squat for beginners who struggle with forward lean. Also useful as a warm-up for experienced lifters.

setup: Stand with feet about hip to shoulder width and hold one dumbbell vertically at your chest, cupping the top end.
Keep your elbows inside your knees and your chest tall so the weight helps you stay upright.
Brace your core and keep the weight centered over your mid-foot before you sit back.
movement: Sit your hips down and back as your knees track over your toes.
Lower until your thighs are about parallel or a comfortable depth.
Drive through your whole foot to stand tall and squeeze your glutes.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your quads doing most of the work, with your core helping you stay stable and upright.

commonFixes.fix: Press your knees out gently and keep your feet rooted. Think: spread the floor, and stay balanced.
Lift your chest and keep your upper back tight. Slow the descent until you can keep posture.

makeItEasier.name: Goblet Squat
makeItEasier.description: Hold a light dumbbell at your chest to practice depth and posture.

levelUp.description: Add a slower 3-second lower and pause at the bottom for extra strength and stability.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you stand. Keep the chest tall.

name: Bodyweight Squat
description: Foundational squat pattern for legs and hips.

setup: Stand balanced with feet about hip to shoulder width.
Brace your core and keep your chest tall.
Reach your arms forward or keep them by your sides for balance.
movement: Sit your hips down and back as your knees track over your toes.
Lower until your thighs are about parallel or a comfortable depth.
Drive through your whole foot to stand tall and squeeze your glutes.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your quads and glutes working evenly, with your core helping you stay stable and upright.

commonFixes.fix: Press your knees out gently and keep your feet rooted. Think: spread the floor, and stay balanced.
Shift your weight back toward mid-foot and heel, and keep your chest tall.

levelUp.description: Add a light dumbbell at your chest for more control and loading.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower into the squat, breathe out as you rise.

name: Kettlebell Deadlift
description: Beginner-friendly hinge with a higher handle.

setup: Place the kettlebell between your feet and stand tall.
Hinge at the hips and keep your back flat.
Grip the handle and brace your core.
movement: Drive through your feet to stand tall.
Squeeze glutes at the top without leaning back.
Lower with control by hinging at the hips.
Reset with the kettlebell under your hips.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your glutes and hamstrings working with a stable lower back.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your chest up and hinge by pushing hips back.
Shift hips back first and keep shins more vertical.

makeItEasier.name: Hip Hinge with Dowel
makeItEasier.description: Practice the hinge without load to learn the pattern.

levelUp.description: Move to a barbell deadlift when you can hinge with control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before the lift, breathe out as you stand tall. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Trap Bar Deadlift
description: Hip-hinge lift with a hex bar; more upright torso than conventional deadlift, often friendlier on the lower back.
siblingReason: Handle height reduces ROM vs barbell. More upright torso reduces lower back stress. Often the better first deadlift for beginners. Also useful during lower back sensitivity periods.

setup: Stand inside the trap bar with feet about hip width, toes slightly out.
Hinge at the hips and grip the handles; keep your spine long and core braced.
Position the bar so it sits evenly; shoulders over or slightly in front of the handles.
movement: Drive through your whole foot and extend hips and knees together.
Stand tall at the top and squeeze your glutes without leaning back.
Lower with control by hinging hips back and bending knees as needed.
Keep the bar close and maintain tension until the next rep.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your glutes and hamstrings driving the lift, with quads helping; your back stays stable and long.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your chest up and hinge at the hips; lower only as far as you can hold a flat back.
Push through the whole foot and keep weight balanced; think about spreading the floor.

makeItEasier.name: Kettlebell Deadlift
makeItEasier.description: Use a kettlebell to learn the hinge pattern with a higher hand position.

levelUp.description: When you want more posterior-chain focus and a longer range of motion, try the conventional barbell deadlift.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower or set up, breathe out as you stand tall. Avoid holding your breath at the hardest point.

name: Rack Pull
description: Shorter-range deadlift to build lockout strength.

setup: Set the bar on safety pins just below knee height.
Hinge at the hips and grip the bar with a flat back.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
movement: Drive through your feet to stand tall.
Squeeze glutes at the top without leaning back.
Lower with control back to the pins.
Reset before the next rep.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel strong glutes and upper back without pulling from the floor.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your chest up and brace hard before you lift.
Build tension first, then drive smoothly upward.

makeItEasier.name: Kettlebell Deadlift
makeItEasier.description: Use a lighter hinge pattern to practice form.

levelUp.description: Move to a full-range deadlift from the floor.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before you lift, breathe out as you stand tall. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Hip Hinge with Dowel
description: Learn the hinge pattern without load.

setup: Place a dowel along your spine touching head, upper back, and tailbone.
Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and knees soft.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
movement: Push your hips back while keeping the dowel in contact with all three points.
Lower until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings.
Drive through your feet to stand tall.
Repeat with slow, controlled reps.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a hip hinge without rounding your back.

commonFixes.fix: Move slower and keep your head, upper back, and tailbone aligned.
Focus on pushing hips back while keeping shins more vertical.

levelUp.description: Progress to a kettlebell deadlift when the hinge feels solid.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you hinge back, breathe out as you stand tall.

name: Romanian Deadlift
description: Targets hamstrings with a controlled hinge.

setup: Start from a standing position with the bar in your hands—unrack it or deadlift it once to get there.
Hinge at the hips, pushing them back and keeping the bar close to your legs throughout the descent.
Keep a soft bend in your knees; they don't drive forward like in a squat—the stretch is in the hamstrings.
movement: Keep the bar close to your legs as you hinge down; you should feel the stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom.
The drive back up comes from your hips pushing forward—distinct from the deadlift, which starts from the floor with a leg drive.
Stand tall and squeeze your glutes at the top, then repeat.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel strong tension in your hamstrings, with your lower-back, core supporting a stable spine.

commonFixes.fix: Hinge at the hips and keep your spine long. Lower only as far as you can hold that shape.
Keep the bar close to your legs and lightly graze your shins and thighs as you move.

makeItEasier.name: Hip Hinge with Dowel
makeItEasier.description: Use a light stick to learn the hinge without load.

levelUp.description: Increase load gradually and add a 1-second squeeze at full hip extension.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you hinge and lower the bar, breathe out as you stand tall.

name: Sumo Deadlift
description: Wide-stance deadlift with more hip and quad drive.
siblingReason: Wider stance reduces torso lean and range of motion. Often better for those with longer torsos, limited hip flexion range, or hip anatomy that restricts conventional stance. Greater glute and adductor emphasis vs conventional.

setup: Set a wide stance with toes turned slightly out.
Grip the bar inside your knees and keep your chest tall.
Brace your core and keep your back flat.
movement: Drive through your feet and push the floor away.
Keep the bar close as you stand tall.
Squeeze glutes at the top without leaning back.
Lower with control and reset.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel strong hip and quad drive with less forward lean than a conventional deadlift.

commonFixes.fix: Press knees out gently to track over toes.
Lift chest and hips together to keep a strong position.

makeItEasier.name: Kettlebell Deadlift
makeItEasier.description: Use a lighter hinge pattern to learn the setup and movement.

levelUp.description: Use sumo when conventional feels back-dominant or when you want more hip/quad emphasis.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before you lift, breathe out as you stand tall. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Deficit Deadlift
description: Increased range to build strength off the floor.

setup: Stand on a small platform so the bar sits lower than normal.
Set up with a flat back and bar over mid-foot.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
movement: Drive through your feet and stand tall.
Keep the bar close as you lift.
Lower under control to the platform.
Reset your brace before the next rep.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel more tension through the hamstrings and off-the-floor strength.

commonFixes.fix: Use a smaller deficit and reduce load to keep a flat back.
Keep the bar close and lightly graze your legs.

makeItEasier.name: Conventional Deadlift
makeItEasier.description: Use the standard range of motion if the deficit breaks form.

levelUp.description: Use when you want more strength off the floor or extra range for long-limbed lifters.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before you lift, breathe out as you stand tall. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Paused Deadlift
description: Pause to build control and strength off the floor.

setup: Set up for a conventional deadlift with a flat back.
Brace your core and keep the bar over mid-foot.
Choose a load you can pause with control.
movement: Lift the bar slightly off the floor and pause for 1–2 seconds.
Keep your back position locked in during the pause.
Drive up to standing smoothly.
Lower with control and reset.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel strong tension through the hamstrings and back while holding position.

commonFixes.fix: Reduce load and shorten the pause until you can hold a flat back.
Pause just off the floor to build the hardest part of the lift.

makeItEasier.name: Conventional Deadlift
makeItEasier.description: Use a standard pull if the pause breaks your position.

levelUp.description: Use when you want more control off the floor or to fix a weak start position.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before you lift, hold tension through the pause, breathe out as you stand tall.

name: Tempo Deadlift
description: Slow lowering to build control and position.

setup: Set up for a conventional deadlift with a flat back.
Brace your core and keep the bar over mid-foot.
Choose a load you can lower slowly with control.
movement: Stand tall with the bar, then lower for a 3-second count.
Keep the bar close to your legs as you hinge.
Maintain a flat back and steady brace.
Reset before the next rep.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel strong control through the hamstrings and back on the way down.

commonFixes.fix: Count out a full 3 seconds and keep tension throughout.
Keep the bar close and lightly graze your legs.

makeItEasier.name: Conventional Deadlift
makeItEasier.description: Use a normal tempo if the slow lowering breaks your position.

levelUp.description: Use when you want more position control or extra time under tension.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before you lift, breathe out as you stand tall. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
description: Single-leg strength and stability builder.
siblingReason: Rear foot elevated increases hip flexor stretch and ROM. Greater single-leg loading than split squat. Not just a harder split squat — the elevated rear foot changes the stimulus meaningfully.

setup: Place the top of your rear foot on the bench behind you and stand far enough forward that your front knee stays over your ankle.
Your front foot should be flat on the floor, roughly two to three feet in front of the bench.
Hold dumbbells at your sides or a single dumbbell in the goblet position; keep your torso upright and core braced.
movement: Lower straight down—don't push your knee forward—so your rear knee travels toward the floor.
Go as deep as you can while keeping your front knee over your ankle and torso upright.
Drive through your front heel to stand back up; repeat on the same leg before switching.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your quads doing most of the work, with your hamstrings, core helping you stay stable and upright.

commonFixes.fix: Take a slightly longer step and keep your weight on your mid-foot and heel.
Slow down and brace your core. Lightly tap your back foot for balance.

makeItEasier.name: Goblet Squat
makeItEasier.description: Hold a light dumbbell at your chest to practice depth and posture.

levelUp.description: Add a slower 3-second lower and pause at the bottom for extra strength and stability.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower your back knee, breathe out as you drive up.

name: Reverse Lunge
description: Step back to reduce knee stress.
siblingReason: Stepping backward is more controlled than forward lunge with less knee shear force at the front knee. Better choice for those with anterior knee pain or early stages of unilateral training.

setup: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
Choose a step length that feels stable.
movement: Step one leg back and lower until both knees are bent.
Keep front knee tracking over toes.
Drive through the front foot to stand tall.
Repeat on the other side.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel quads and glutes working with less knee stress than a forward lunge.

commonFixes.fix: Press the knee out gently and keep the foot rooted.
Keep chest lifted and torso tall throughout.

makeItEasier.name: Supported Split Squat
makeItEasier.description: Hold a support for balance while you build control.

levelUp.description: Add load with dumbbells or progress to a walking lunge.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you stand. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Split Squat
description: Stationary lunge for control and balance.
siblingReason: Static split — no step. Easier to learn than lunge because balance is not disrupted by movement. Best starting point for unilateral loading.

setup: Take a split stance with one foot forward and one foot back.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
Set your feet far enough apart to feel stable.
movement: Lower straight down until both knees are bent.
Keep your front knee tracking over toes.
Drive through the front foot to stand tall.
Repeat without changing your stance.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel quads and glutes working with steady balance.

commonFixes.fix: Widen your stance and slow the movement.
Press the knee out gently and keep the foot rooted.

makeItEasier.name: Supported Split Squat
makeItEasier.description: Hold onto a wall, rack, or chair for balance until the pattern feels stable.

levelUp.description: Add load with dumbbells or progress to a Bulgarian split squat.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you stand. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Step-Up
description: Single-leg strength using a box or bench.
siblingReason: Concentric-focused unilateral work. Load through the working leg only. Good carry-over to stair climbing and daily function. Often used in knee rehab protocols.

setup: Stand facing a sturdy box or bench.
Place one foot fully on the box and brace your core.
Keep your chest lifted and hips square.
movement: Drive through the front foot to step up.
Stand tall without pushing off the back foot.
Lower with control and repeat on the same side.
Switch sides after completing reps.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your front leg doing most of the work with stable balance.

commonFixes.fix: Use the back foot lightly and drive through the front leg.
Press the knee out gently and keep the foot rooted.

makeItEasier.name: Reverse Lunge
makeItEasier.description: Use a step-back lunge if the box height is too challenging.

levelUp.description: Add dumbbells or increase box height for more challenge.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you step up. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Front-Foot Elevated Split Squat
description: Elevate the front foot to increase range.

setup: Place your front foot on a low box or plate.
Set your back foot behind you in a split stance.
Brace your core and keep your chest tall.
movement: Lower straight down until both knees are bent.
Drive through the elevated front foot to stand tall.
Keep your torso upright and hips square.
Repeat on the same side, then switch.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel more quad stretch and control than a standard split squat.

commonFixes.fix: Press the knee out gently and keep the foot rooted.
Keep your chest lifted and torso tall.

makeItEasier.name: Split Squat
makeItEasier.description: Use a standard split squat if the range feels too deep.

levelUp.description: Add load with dumbbells once you can control the full range.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you stand. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Lateral Lunge
description: Side-to-side lunge for hip strength.
siblingReason: Frontal plane movement. Trains adductors and hip abductors under load. Cannot be substituted for sagittal plane lunges — it targets genuinely different muscles. Use when adductor strength or hip mobility in the frontal plane is a goal.

setup: Stand tall with feet wider than hip-width.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
Shift your weight to one side before stepping.
movement: Step out to the side and sit your hips back.
Keep the working knee tracking over toes.
Drive through the working foot to return to center.
Repeat on the other side.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your inner thigh and glutes working with added hip mobility.

commonFixes.fix: Press the knee out gently and keep the foot rooted.
Keep your chest lifted and hinge at the hips.

makeItEasier.name: Assisted Lateral Lunge
makeItEasier.description: Use a wall or rack for support while building mobility.

levelUp.description: Add load with a dumbbell or widen stance for more hip stretch.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you return. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Cossack Squat
description: Deep lateral squat for hip mobility.

setup: Stand tall with a wide stance and toes slightly out.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
Shift your weight to one side before lowering.
movement: Sit your hips back and down on one side.
Keep the working knee tracking over toes.
Keep the other leg long and foot up if mobility allows.
Drive through the working foot to return to center.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a deep hip and adductor stretch with controlled strength.

commonFixes.issue: Heel lifting on the working side
commonFixes.fix: Widen your stance and keep weight over the mid-foot.
Keep chest lifted and hinge at the hips.

makeItEasier.name: Lateral Lunge
makeItEasier.description: Use a shallower range of motion before moving to a deep cossack.

levelUp.description: Add a light dumbbell or go deeper as mobility improves.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you return. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Side-Lying Adduction
description: Low-load adductor work for hip stability.

setup: Lie on your side with the bottom leg straight.
Cross the top leg over and place the foot flat on the floor.
Brace your core and keep hips stacked.
movement: Lift the bottom leg toward the ceiling with control.
Pause briefly at the top.
Lower slowly to the start without resting.
Repeat on both sides.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a gentle burn in the inner thigh without hip strain.

commonFixes.fix: Keep hips stacked and core braced.
Slow the tempo and keep the movement controlled.

makeItEasier.name: Cossack Squat
makeItEasier.description: Use a deeper squat pattern if you want a compound version.

levelUp.description: Add a light ankle weight once control feels easy.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you lift. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Side-Lying Abduction
description: Glute med work for hip stability.

setup: Lie on your side with the top leg straight and hips stacked.
Brace your core and keep your pelvis steady.
Point toes forward or slightly down.
movement: Lift the top leg up with control.
Pause briefly at the top.
Lower slowly without swinging.
Repeat on both sides.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a burn in the side of the hip and glute without low-back strain.

commonFixes.fix: Keep hips stacked and core braced.
Point toes forward to keep the glutes working.

makeItEasier.name: Lateral Lunge
makeItEasier.description: Use a compound version if you want more global lower-body work.

levelUp.description: Add a light ankle weight once control feels easy.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you lift. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Slideboard Lunge
description: Low-impact lateral lunge with control.

setup: Stand on a slideboard or towels on a smooth floor.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
Place one foot on the sliding surface.
movement: Slide one foot out to the side as you sit your hips back.
Keep the working knee tracking over toes.
Drive through the working foot to return to center.
Repeat on both sides.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel adductors and glutes working with smooth, controlled movement.

commonFixes.fix: Press the knee out gently and keep the foot rooted.
Slow down and shorten the range until you can control it.

makeItEasier.name: Lateral Lunge
makeItEasier.description: Use a standard lateral lunge if sliding is too unstable.

levelUp.description: Add a light dumbbell or increase range as control improves.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you return. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Standing Cable Hip Abduction
description: Isolated abductor work for hip stability.

setup: Attach an ankle strap to a low cable and stand tall.
Hold the machine for balance and brace your core.
Keep hips square and toes pointing forward.
movement: Move the working leg out to the side with control.
Pause briefly at the top.
Return slowly without swinging.
Repeat on both sides.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel the side of the hip working without low-back strain.

commonFixes.fix: Stand tall and let the hip do the work.
Slow down and keep the movement controlled.

makeItEasier.name: Side-Lying Abduction
makeItEasier.description: Use a bodyweight version if the cable load is too much.

levelUp.description: Increase load slightly once you can control the full range.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you return, breathe out as you lift. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Standing Cable Hip Adduction
description: Isolated adductor work for hip strength.

setup: Attach an ankle strap to a low cable and stand tall.
Hold the machine for balance and brace your core.
Keep hips square and toes pointing forward.
movement: Move the working leg across your body with control.
Pause briefly at the end range.
Return slowly without swinging.
Repeat on both sides.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel the inner thigh working without low-back strain.

commonFixes.fix: Stand tall and keep your core braced.
Slow down and keep the movement controlled.

makeItEasier.name: Side-Lying Adduction
makeItEasier.description: Use a bodyweight version if the cable load is too much.

levelUp.description: Increase load slightly once you can control the full range.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you return, breathe out as you pull. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Weighted Lateral Lunge
description: Loaded side lunge for stronger hips.

setup: Hold a dumbbell at your chest or by your sides.
Stand tall with feet wider than hip-width.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
movement: Step out to the side and sit your hips back.
Keep the working knee tracking over toes.
Drive through the working foot to return to center.
Repeat on both sides.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel strong inner thigh and glute engagement with added load.

commonFixes.fix: Press the knee out gently and keep the foot rooted.
Keep your chest lifted and hinge at the hips.

makeItEasier.name: Lateral Lunge
makeItEasier.description: Use the bodyweight version until balance feels solid.

levelUp.description: Increase load or deepen range as strength improves.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you return. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Step-Down
description: Controlled single-leg lowering for knee tracking.
siblingReason: Eccentric-focused. Controls the lowering phase on one leg. Excellent for patellar tendon and VMO strengthening. Often prescribed post knee surgery or for patellofemoral pain.

setup: Stand on a box with one foot near the edge.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted.
Keep hips square and the working knee tracking forward.
movement: Lower slowly until the free heel taps the floor.
Keep the working knee tracking over toes.
Drive through the working foot to stand tall.
Repeat on both sides.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel controlled quad and glute work with stable knee tracking.

commonFixes.fix: Press the knee out gently and keep the foot rooted.
Slow down and control the lowering phase.

makeItEasier.name: Split Squat
makeItEasier.description: Use a stationary split squat if the step feels too unstable.

levelUp.description: Add a dumbbell or increase the step height as control improves.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you stand. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Leg Press Machine
description: Load the legs safely with back support.
siblingReason: Allows significant quad loading without balance, upper body, or core stability demands. Appropriate when isolating leg development, during upper body injury rehab, or as an early-stage option for those not yet confident with free weight squatting.

setup: Set your body position first: feet planted, core tight, shoulders down and back.
Grip the leg press machine so your wrists stay straight and stacked.
Keep your elbows under the weight so your forearms stay vertical.
movement: Unlock the sled and lower by bending your knees until they're at a comfortable depth—don't let your lower back round.
Push the sled away by extending your legs, driving through your whole foot.
Finish with legs extended but keep a slight bend in the knees at the top; don't lock out hard.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your quads working hard, with the hamstrings assisting through the press.

commonFixes.fix: Extend fully but keep a soft knee at the top so you stay in control.
Aim for a mid-foot position so your knees track comfortably over your toes.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower the sled, breathe out as you press back to the start.

name: Leg Extension Machine
description: Isolates the quads with simple form.

setup: Sit with your back flat against the pad and adjust the seat so the pivot lines up with your knee.
Position the shin pad so it rests on the front of your ankles, just below the shin.
Grip the handles at the sides and keep your hips and back firmly against the pad.
movement: Extend your lower legs until your knees are straight, keeping your thighs and hips still.
Pause at the top and squeeze your quads.
Lower under control back to the start and repeat.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel the quads doing the work, not your shoulders.

commonFixes.fix: Pause at the top and lower slowly. Control matters more than load.
Adjust the pad on your lower shins and keep your hips down.

makeItEasier.name: Light Weight Version
makeItEasier.description: Reduce the load and slow the tempo so you can feel the target muscle working.

levelUp.description: Try a slower 3-second lowering phase to increase time under tension.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower the weight, breathe out as you extend your legs.

name: Lying Leg Curl
description: Direct hamstring work with controlled reps.
siblingReason: Prone position puts hip into extension, reducing rectus femoris contribution and increasing hamstring isolation. The stretched position under load (fully extended) provides a different mechanical stimulus.

setup: Lie face down on the machine with the ankle pad sitting just above your heels.
Keep your hips flat on the bench; adjust the thigh pad so you can't lift your hips off the pad.
Use a light grip on the handles in front of you for stability.
movement: Curl your heels toward your glutes with control; keep your hips pressed into the bench.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze your hamstrings.
Lower slowly to the start—don't let the weight stack crash down.
instructions.goodFormFeels: Your hamstrings should be doing the work, and your hips should stay flat on the bench.

commonFixes.fix: Press your hips down into the pad and reduce the weight if you can't keep them down.
Slow the tempo—especially on the lowering phase—and use a lighter load if needed.

makeItEasier.name: Light Weight Version
makeItEasier.description: Reduce the load and slow the tempo so you can feel the target muscle working.

levelUp.description: Try a slower 3-second lowering phase to increase time under tension.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe out as you curl up, breathe in as you lower.

name: Seated Leg Curl
description: Seated hamstring curl with stable setup.
siblingReason: Hip flexed position increases hamstring stretch at the hip. Research suggests greater hamstring hypertrophy stimulus due to stretch under load. The two machines provide genuinely different hamstring stimuli and ideally both are used.

setup: Adjust the machine so your knees align with the pivot.
Sit tall with your back supported and core braced.
Set the pad just above your ankles.
movement: Curl your heels toward your glutes with control.
Pause briefly at the end range.
Lower slowly to a full stretch.
Repeat without bouncing.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a strong hamstring contraction without low-back strain.

commonFixes.fix: Slow down and pause briefly at the top.
Keep hips and back firmly against the pad.

makeItEasier.name: Lying Leg Curl
makeItEasier.description: Use a lying curl if the seated machine is unavailable.

levelUp.description: Increase load or add a longer pause at the top.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you curl. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Standing Calf Raise
description: Builds strong calves and ankle stiffness.
siblingReason: Knee extended — targets gastrocnemius primarily. The larger, more visible calf muscle.

setup: Place the balls of your feet on the platform with heels free to drop.
Stand tall and lightly hold the handles for balance, not for support.
Brace your core and keep your knees softly unlocked.
movement: Lower your heels until you feel a stretch in your calves.
Pause briefly at the bottom to remove momentum.
Press through the big toe and rise as high as you can.
Hold the top squeeze for a second, then lower slowly.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a strong contraction in the calves and a steady stretch at the bottom. Focus on range and control over heavy weight.

commonFixes.fix: Pause briefly in the stretched position to build strength through range.
Let your heel drop below the step before rising all the way up.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower your heels, breathe out as you rise onto your toes.

name: Seated Calf Raise
description: Seated calf work to target the soleus.
siblingReason: Knee bent — gastrocnemius is slackened, targeting soleus. A deeper, postural muscle. Both are needed for full calf development — they cannot substitute for each other.

setup: Sit tall with knees bent and feet on the platform.
Place the pad over your thighs and brace your core.
Start from a full stretch at the bottom.
movement: Press through the balls of your feet to rise up.
Pause briefly at the top.
Lower under control to a full stretch.
Repeat without bouncing.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a burn in the calves without ankle strain.

commonFixes.fix: Pause briefly at the stretch and move with control.
Slow down and use a full range of motion.

makeItEasier.name: Standing Calf Raise
makeItEasier.description: Use a bodyweight standing raise if the machine is unavailable.

levelUp.description: Increase load or add a longer pause at the top.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you rise. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Walking Lunge
description: Leg strength plus balance in motion.
siblingReason: Adds coordination of continuous stepping. Greater hip flexor stretch through the stride. More metabolic demand. Not a regression or progression of reverse lunge — a different stimulus.

setup: Stand balanced with feet about hip to shoulder width.
Brace your core and keep your chest tall.
Hold the bodyweight securely with weight centered over mid-foot.
movement: Step forward into a lunge and lower your rear knee toward the floor.
Drive through your front foot to stand and step through, bringing the back foot forward into the next lunge.
Stay tall through the torso and keep the movement smooth—each rep is one step forward.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your quads doing most of the work, with your hamstrings, core helping you stay stable and upright.

commonFixes.fix: Take a slightly longer step and keep your weight on your mid-foot and heel.
Slow down and brace your core. Lightly tap your back foot for balance.

makeItEasier.name: Goblet Squat
makeItEasier.description: Hold a light dumbbell at your chest to practice depth and posture.

levelUp.description: Add a slower 3-second lower and pause at the bottom for extra strength and stability.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you step down into the lunge, breathe out as you drive up and step through.

name: Hip Thrust
description: Glute strength with a strong lockout.

setup: Position your upper back on the bench edge so your shoulder blades are supported and your hips can move freely.
Place your feet flat on the floor about hip width apart with knees bent.
Rest the barbell across your hips with a pad for comfort; tuck your chin slightly and brace your core.
movement: Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes until your body forms a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Pause briefly at the top with hips level—avoid over-arching your lower back.
Lower with control toward the start position and repeat.
instructions.goodFormFeels: Your glutes should do the work—your lower back should not take over.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your ribs down and squeeze your glutes at the top instead of over-arching.
Set your feet so your shins are vertical at the top.

makeItEasier.name: Hip Hinge with Dowel
makeItEasier.description: Use a light stick to learn the hinge without load.

levelUp.description: Increase load gradually and add a 1-second squeeze at full hip extension.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in at the bottom, breathe out as you drive your hips up.

name: Barbell Bench Press
description: The classic chest press for strength.

setup: Lie on the bench with your eyes under the bar and a slight arch in your upper back.
Grip the bar about one and a half times shoulder width so your forearms stay vertical at the bottom.
Plant your feet and squeeze your shoulder blades together before you unrack.
movement: Lower the weight in a controlled path toward your chest or shoulders.
Pause briefly without losing tension.
Press up smoothly, driving through your full hand.
Finish with arms straight but not hyper-extended.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your chest working hard, with the triceps, front-shoulders assisting through the press.

commonFixes.fix: Pin your shoulder blades together and keep them there.
Tuck elbows slightly so your forearms stay vertical.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Once you can complete all reps smoothly, add a 2-second pause at the bottom to build control and strength.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower the bar to your chest, breathe out as you press it up.

name: Incline Bench Press
description: Targets upper chest with a slight incline.
siblingReason: Upper pec and anterior delt emphasis. Slightly more shoulder-friendly for some due to reduced range at shoulder impingement angle. Not interchangeable with flat bench — a different stimulus for upper chest development.

setup: Set the bench to a low incline and plant your feet firmly.
Grip the barbell so your wrists stay straight and stacked.
Keep shoulders down and back with your chest lifted.
movement: Lower the bar toward your upper chest in a controlled path.
Pause briefly without losing tension.
Press up smoothly, driving through your full hand.
Finish with arms straight but not hyper-extended.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel the upper chest working hard, with triceps and front-shoulders assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Pin your shoulder blades together and keep them there.
Tuck elbows slightly so your forearms stay vertical.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second pause at the bottom to build control and strength.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you press. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Decline Bench Press
description: Lower-chest emphasis with a stable press path.
siblingReason: Lower pec emphasis. Reduces shoulder stress for some. Less commonly programmed — most programmes achieve sufficient lower pec stimulus from flat pressing. Include when specifically targeting lower pec fullness.

setup: Set the bench to a gentle decline and secure your legs.
Grip the barbell so your wrists stay straight and stacked.
Keep shoulders down and back with your chest lifted.
movement: Lower the bar toward your lower chest in a controlled path.
Pause briefly without losing tension.
Press up smoothly, driving through your full hand.
Finish with arms straight but not hyper-extended.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel the lower chest working hard, with triceps and front-shoulders assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Pin your shoulder blades together and keep them there.
Tuck elbows slightly so your forearms stay vertical.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second pause at the bottom to build control and strength.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you press. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Chest Press Machine
description: Stable chest press with guided path.
siblingReason: Guided path removes stability demand entirely. Appropriate for early-stage beginners, those rebuilding after injury, or when isolating chest stimulus without fatigue affecting technique.

setup: Adjust the seat so handles start at chest height.
Place your feet flat and brace your core.
Keep shoulders down and back.
movement: Press the handles forward in a smooth path.
Stop just short of locking out hard.
Return with control to the start.
Repeat without letting shoulders roll forward.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a strong chest contraction with stable shoulders.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your shoulder blades lightly pinned back.
Keep elbows slightly tucked as you press.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use a bodyweight press if the machine is unavailable.

levelUp.description: Move to barbell or dumbbell bench press for more stability demand.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you return, breathe out as you press. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
description: Press with a natural arm path.
siblingReason: Each arm works independently, which exposes and corrects strength imbalances. Greater shoulder-friendly range of motion at the bottom. The rotating grip reduces shoulder impingement risk. Often the better choice for those with shoulder history or asymmetry.

setup: Sit with a dumbbell on each knee, then lie back and bring the weights to chest height at your sides.
Use a neutral or pronated grip with your wrists straight and elbows under the weights.
Plant your feet and squeeze your shoulder blades so your chest is open.
movement: Lower the weight in a controlled path toward your chest or shoulders.
Pause briefly without losing tension.
Press up smoothly, driving through your full hand.
Finish with arms straight but not hyper-extended.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your chest working hard, with the triceps, front-shoulders assisting through the press.

commonFixes.fix: Pin your shoulder blades together and keep them there.
Tuck elbows slightly so your forearms stay vertical.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Once you can complete all reps smoothly, add a 2-second pause at the bottom to build control and strength.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower the dumbbells, breathe out as you press them up.

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
description: Targets the upper chest and shoulders.
siblingReason: Combines incline upper pec emphasis with dumbbell independence benefits. Often the most shoulder-friendly pressing variation. Good choice when shoulder health is a priority alongside upper chest development.

setup: Set the bench to 30 to 45 degrees and lie back with your whole back and head on the pad.
Bring the dumbbells to shoulder height with your elbows slightly below your wrists.
Keep your feet flat and core braced so you don't slide down the bench.
movement: Lower the weight in a controlled path toward your upper chest.
Press up smoothly, driving through your full hand.
Finish with arms straight but not hyper-extended.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your upper-chest working hard, with the triceps, front-shoulders assisting through the press.

commonFixes.fix: Keep shoulders down and back. Think: long neck as you press.
Stop when your elbows are about level with the bench.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Once you can complete all reps smoothly, add a 2-second pause at the bottom to build control and strength.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower the weights, breathe out as you press up.

name: Wall Push-Up
description: Easiest push-up regression to build pressing strength.

setup: Stand facing a wall about an arm's length away.
Place hands on the wall at chest height, slightly wider than shoulders.
Brace your core and keep a straight line from head to heels.
movement: Bend your elbows and bring your chest toward the wall.
Keep elbows at a comfortable angle, not flared straight out.
Press the wall away to return to the start.
Reset without locking out hard.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your chest and triceps working while your core stays tight.

commonFixes.fix: Keep a straight line from head to heels and squeeze your glutes.

commonFixes.issue: Hands too high or too low
commonFixes.fix: Set hands at chest height so the press feels natural and controlled.

levelUp.description: Move to an incline push-up on a bench to increase the load.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you press away. Keep breathing steady.

name: Push-Up
description: A full-body press you can do anywhere.

setup: Place your hands about shoulder width apart, fingers pointing forward or slightly out.
Get into a strong plank with your body in one line from head to heels.
Pack your shoulders down and away from your ears before you lower.
movement: Lower slowly until you feel a stretch across the chest.
Keep elbows at a comfortable angle, not straight out to the sides.
Press back up with control, keeping your body rigid.
Reset at the top without locking out hard.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a deep chest contraction with your triceps, front-shoulders, core assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Squeeze glutes and keep a straight line from head to heels.
Keep elbows about 30 to 45 degrees from your body.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Once you can complete all reps smoothly, add a 2-second pause at the bottom to build control and strength.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower your chest, breathe out as you push back up.

name: Weighted Push-Up
description: Progress the push-up with extra load.
siblingReason: Allows progressive overload of the push-up pattern without a rack. Good for home gym or travel training. Also useful for adding load to push-up as a secondary exercise after heavier bench work.

setup: Set your hands so your wrists feel comfortable and your body is rigid.
Brace your core and keep a straight line from head to heels.
Place a light plate or backpack on your upper back for added load.
movement: Lower with control until you feel a stretch across the chest.
Keep elbows at a comfortable angle, not straight out to the sides.
Press back up smoothly while keeping your body rigid.
Reset at the top without locking out hard.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a strong chest contraction with triceps and core assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Squeeze glutes and keep a straight line from head to heels.
Keep elbows about 30 to 45 degrees from your body.

makeItEasier.name: Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use bodyweight only until you can hold perfect form.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second pause at the bottom or increase load slightly.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you press. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Ring Push-Up
description: Unstable push-up that builds control and strength.
siblingReason: Unstable surface dramatically increases stabiliser demand across chest, shoulders, and serratus anterior. Not a simple progression of push-up — a qualitatively harder movement that also improves shoulder joint stability and proprioception.

setup: Set rings so they hang just above the floor.
Grab the rings and brace your core in a straight-body plank.
Keep shoulders packed down and back.
movement: Lower with control until you feel a deep chest stretch.
Keep elbows at a comfortable angle, not flared straight out.
Press back up smoothly while keeping the rings stable.
Reset at the top without locking out hard.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your chest working hard with extra core and shoulder stability demands.

commonFixes.fix: Slow down and squeeze the rings together slightly for control.
Squeeze glutes and keep a straight line from head to heels.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Add load with a light plate or increase range of motion gradually.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you press. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Cable Chest Fly
description: Isolate the chest with smooth tension.

setup: Set both cables to about chest height and stand in the centre in a split stance for balance.
Start with arms wide to the sides, elbows softly bent and wrists in line with your forearms.
Lean slightly forward from the hips so your chest leads and you feel a stretch in the pecs.
movement: Bring your hands together in a wide arc in front of your chest, keeping that soft elbow bend constant.
Squeeze your chest at the peak of the movement.
Open back slowly with control—resist the stretch on the way out.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a chest stretch as you open, then a deep chest squeeze where your hands meet.

commonFixes.fix: Keep a soft bend in your elbows throughout the whole rep.
Maintain a slight hip hinge so you're not standing bolt upright.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as your arms open wide, breathe out as you bring them together.

name: Incline Push-Up
description: An easier push-up with clean form.

setup: Place your hands on a bench, box or wall at a height that keeps the move manageable.
Walk your feet back until your body is in a straight line from head to heels.
Hands about shoulder width, shoulders packed down, core braced.
movement: Lower your chest toward the bench or surface until you feel a stretch through the chest.
Keep elbows at a comfortable angle—about 30 to 45 degrees from your body—not flared to 90.
Press your body away with control, maintaining one straight line from head to heels.
Finish with arms straight without snapping your elbows.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a deep chest contraction with your triceps, front-shoulders, core assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Squeeze glutes and keep a straight line from head to heels.
Keep elbows about 30 to 45 degrees from your body.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Once you can complete all reps smoothly, add a 2-second pause at the bottom to build control and strength.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower your chest toward the surface, breathe out as you push up.

name: Dips (Chest-Focused)
description: Chest-biased dip for powerful pressing.

setup: Grip the bars with a hand width that feels comfortable and secure, usually slightly wider than shoulder width.
Support your weight with arms straight and lean your torso forward to bias the chest.
Keep your shoulders down and core braced before you lower.
movement: Lower yourself between the bars with control, keeping the forward lean so your chest stays loaded.
Descend until you feel a comfortable stretch across your chest or reach your safe depth.
Press back up by straightening your elbows and squeezing your chest at the top.
Avoid bouncing out of the bottom—each rep should be smooth and controlled.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a deep chest contraction with your triceps, front-shoulders assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Keep shoulders down and chest proud throughout the dip.
Lean slightly forward so your chest takes more load.

makeItEasier.name: Incline Push-Up
makeItEasier.description: Raise your hands on a bench to reduce load while keeping the same pressing pattern.

levelUp.description: Once you can complete all reps smoothly, add a 2-second pause at the bottom to build control and strength.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower your chest, breathe out as you drive back up.

name: Lat Pulldown
description: Builds a strong, wide back.
siblingReason: Machine-guided, load easily adjusted. Primary beginner vertical pull. Important also as a volume tool for intermediate/advanced lifters after pull-ups — allows higher rep ranges with reduced fatigue.

setup: Adjust the seat pad so your thighs are firmly under the rollers and you can reach the bar comfortably.
Grip the bar at your chosen width—wider for more lat focus—and sit with a slight lean back.
Pull your shoulders down and back before you pull so your lats are engaged from the start.
movement: Drive your elbows down or back as you pull.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end.
Pause briefly, then return slowly to full stretch.
Keep your chest lifted the whole time.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your lats and upper back working, with biceps, upper-back assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Pull the bar to the upper chest to keep shoulders safe.
Sit tall and pull with your lats, not momentum.

makeItEasier.name: Assisted Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use assistance or a band to reduce body weight while keeping full range.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second squeeze at the top of each rep to build upper-back control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you let the bar rise, breathe out as you pull the bar down.

name: Pull-Up
description: A classic bodyweight back builder.

setup: Hang from the bar with arms fully extended—dead hang—and shoulders relaxed up by your ears to start.
Use an overhand grip about shoulder width or slightly wider, depending on what feels strong.
Engage your lats and core so your body is slightly hollow before you pull.
movement: Drive your elbows down or back as you pull.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end.
Pause briefly, then return slowly to full stretch.
Keep your chest lifted the whole time.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your lats and upper back working, with biceps, upper-back assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Start from a dead hang and pull until your chin clears the bar.
Cross your ankles and pause briefly at the bottom.

makeItEasier.name: Assisted Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use assistance or a band to reduce body weight while keeping full range.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second squeeze at the top of each rep to build upper-back control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower with control, breathe out as you pull your chin over the bar.

name: Neutral-Grip Pull-Up
description: Shoulder-friendly pull-up variation.
siblingReason: Most shoulder-friendly pull-up grip. Reduces shoulder impingement risk. Often the best choice for those with shoulder sensitivity or returning from shoulder issues. Intermediate difficulty between lat pulldown and pronated pull-up.

setup: Grip the neutral handles and hang tall with shoulders engaged.
Pull your shoulders down to engage your lats.
Keep your core tight to avoid swinging.
movement: Drive your elbows down as you pull.
Bring your chin to handle height.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower with control.
Return to a full hang without losing tension.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your lats working with less shoulder stress than a standard pull-up.

commonFixes.fix: Cross ankles and pause at the bottom to regain control.
Start from a full hang and finish with chin to handle height.

makeItEasier.name: Band-Assisted Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use a band to keep the full range of motion with less load.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second squeeze at the top to build upper-back control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you pull. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Chin-Up
description: Underhand pull-up for back and biceps.
siblingReason: Supinated grip increases bicep contribution, making it easier for most people than a pull-up. Often the appropriate first bodyweight vertical pull. Also specifically useful when bicep development is a goal alongside back work.

setup: Hang from the bar in a dead hang with arms straight and shoulders relaxed.
Use an underhand grip about shoulder width apart, palms facing you.
Brace your core and pull your shoulders down slightly before you begin the pull.
movement: Drive your elbows down and back as you pull; the supinated grip brings your biceps into the move more than a pull-up.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together and pull until your chin clears the bar.
Lower with control to a full hang and repeat.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your lats and biceps working with your upper back supporting the pull.

commonFixes.fix: Start from a dead hang and pull until your chin clears the bar.
Cross your ankles and pause briefly at the bottom.

makeItEasier.name: Assisted Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use assistance or a band to reduce body weight while keeping full range.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second squeeze at the top of each rep to build upper-back control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you pull your chin over the bar.

name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
description: Targets the whole back with a hinge.

setup: Hinge at the hips until your torso is about 45 degrees to the floor, knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar just outside your legs—about shoulder width or slightly wider—and let it hang beneath your shoulders.
Keep your back flat, core braced and chest open so you're ready to pull the bar to your belly.
movement: Row the bar toward your lower ribs or upper belly in a horizontal path—elbows drive back toward your hips, not straight down like a pulldown.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top without jerking your torso.
Lower the bar with straight arms until you feel a stretch across your upper back.
Keep your hinge angle and spinal position steady from rep to rep.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your upper back and lats working, with your biceps helping and your lower back stable.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your chest up and pull your shoulders back at the end.
Keep your torso still and move the weight with your back.

makeItEasier.name: Assisted Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use assistance or a band to reduce body weight while keeping full range.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second squeeze at the top of each rep to build upper-back control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower the bar, breathe out as you row it to your belly.

name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row
description: Row without loading the lower back.
siblingReason: Chest support removes lower back from the equation entirely. Best choice when lower back fatigue or sensitivity is present, or when isolating upper back stimulus without spinal erector involvement.

setup: Set an incline bench and lie chest-down with feet planted.
Hold dumbbells with a neutral grip and brace your core.
Keep your neck long and shoulders packed.
movement: Row the dumbbells toward your hips with control.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower slowly until arms are fully extended.
Repeat without bouncing off the bench.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your upper back and lats working without lower-back strain.

commonFixes.fix: Keep shoulders down and back; think 'long neck' as you row.
Slow the movement and pause briefly at the top.

makeItEasier.name: Resistance Band Row
makeItEasier.description: Use a band to learn the row pattern with lighter load.

levelUp.description: Move to a barbell row when you want more load and full-body stability.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you row. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Resistance Band Row
description: Simple row with minimal equipment.

setup: Anchor a band at chest height and hold one end in each hand.
Step back to create light tension and brace your core.
Stand tall with shoulders down and back.
movement: Pull the band toward your ribs with control.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top.
Slowly return to the start with tension on the band.
Repeat without shrugging.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your upper back and lats working without lower-back strain.

commonFixes.fix: Keep shoulders down and away from your ears.
Stay tall and let the band tension do the work.

levelUp.description: Move to a chest-supported dumbbell row for more load and stability.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you return, breathe out as you row. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Inverted Row
description: Bodyweight row for home or gym setups.

setup: Set a bar or suspension trainer at waist height.
Grab the handles or bar and walk feet forward.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels.
movement: Row your chest toward the bar/handles.
Squeeze shoulder blades at the top.
Lower with control to a full stretch.
Repeat without letting hips sag.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your upper back and lats working with steady core tension.

commonFixes.fix: Squeeze glutes and keep a straight line.
Keep shoulders down and pull elbows back.

makeItEasier.name: Resistance Band Row
makeItEasier.description: Use a lighter banded row if bodyweight is too challenging.

levelUp.description: Walk feet further forward or elevate feet for more load.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you row. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Pendlay Row
description: Explosive row from the floor with strict resets.
siblingReason: Dead stop from floor each rep eliminates momentum. More explosive and technically demanding than standard bent-over row. Greater upper back and trap emphasis. Used for power development and strict strength — not a simple harder version of bent-over row.

setup: Hinge at the hips and set your back flat with the bar over mid-foot.
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width.
Brace your core and keep your neck long.
movement: Pull the bar explosively toward your lower chest.
Let the bar return to the floor and fully reset each rep.
Keep your torso fixed; avoid rising as you pull.
Repeat with crisp reps.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel powerful upper-back engagement with strict, repeatable reps.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your chest over the bar and hinge angle fixed.
Pause and reset before each rep for strict form.

makeItEasier.name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
makeItEasier.description: Use a controlled row without floor resets to learn the pattern.

levelUp.description: Add load, use a paused 1-second hold at the top, or stand on a small platform to increase range of motion.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before the pull, exhale as you row. Reset your brace each rep.

name: Dumbbell Single-Arm Row
description: Unilateral row for back balance.
siblingReason: Greater ROM possible vs chest-supported due to slight hip shift. Unilateral — catches and corrects strength imbalances. Good choice when asymmetry is suspected or when full range of motion is the priority.

setup: Place one knee and the same-side hand on the bench; keep your other foot flat on the floor.
Hinge so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor and the dumbbell hangs beneath your shoulder.
Keep your back flat and core braced so you don't rotate as you row.
movement: Drive your elbows down or back as you pull.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end.
Pause briefly, then return slowly to full stretch.
Keep your chest lifted the whole time.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your lats and upper back working, with upper-back, biceps assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your chest up and pull your shoulders back at the end.
Keep your torso still and move the weight with your back.

makeItEasier.name: Assisted Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use assistance or a band to reduce body weight while keeping full range.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second squeeze at the top of each rep to build upper-back control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower the dumbbell, breathe out as you row it to your hip.

name: Cable Seated Row
description: Smooth horizontal pull with constant tension.
siblingReason: Constant tension throughout range of motion due to cable. Machine guidance is helpful for beginners. Good volume tool. Different resistance profile to free weights — useful for variety and hypertrophy.

setup: Sit with feet on the platform, knees slightly bent, and grasp the handle with both hands.
Sit tall with a slight forward lean, arms extended, and feel tension in the cable before you pull.
Keep your chest up and shoulders down so you row into your mid or upper belly.
movement: Pull the handle toward your lower ribs or upper belly in a horizontal row—elbows travel back, not down toward the floor.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of the pull without rocking your torso.
Straighten your arms slowly and feel a stretch across your upper back before the next rep.
Stay tall through the chest and keep your hips and feet fixed on the platform.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your upper back and lats working, with your biceps assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Keep your chest up and pull your shoulders back at the end.
Keep your torso still and move the weight with your back.

makeItEasier.name: Assisted Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use assistance or a band to reduce body weight while keeping full range.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second squeeze at the top of each rep to build upper-back control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you let the handle return, breathe out as you pull it to your stomach.

name: Landmine Row
description: Single-arm landmine row for lat focus and lower-back-friendly loading.
siblingReason: Angled cable path reduces lower back stress vs standard bent-over row. Good choice for those with lower back sensitivity who still want barbell rowing stimulus. Also useful as a unilateral variation with single-arm grip.

setup: Set one end of a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner.
Hinge at the hips and keep your back flat with chest supported by your brace.
Grip the bar near the sleeve with one hand and brace your core.
movement: Row the bar toward your hip with control.
Squeeze your shoulder blade at the top.
Lower slowly to full stretch.
Repeat without twisting your torso.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a strong lat stretch and solid upper-back engagement without lower-back strain.

commonFixes.fix: Square your hips and keep your chest steady throughout the row.
Keep shoulders down and pull your elbow toward your hip.

makeItEasier.name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row
makeItEasier.description: Use bench support to reduce bracing demands while learning the row pattern.

levelUp.description: Use a Meadows-row stance and grip to increase lat stretch and challenge control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you row. Keep shoulders relaxed and avoid holding your breath.

name: Face Pull
description: Great for posture and shoulder health.

setup: Set the cable to face height and grab the rope or handles with a neutral grip, thumbs back.
Step back until your arms are straight and there's light tension on the cable.
Stand tall with a slight bend in your elbows and your elbows level with your shoulders.
movement: Pull the rope toward your face with your elbows staying high and wide—not driving down like a row.
Finish with your hands beside your ears and your elbows out to the sides.
Squeeze your rear delts and upper back at the peak, then return slowly to the start.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel your rear-delts and upper back working, with upper-back, rotator-cuff assisting.

commonFixes.fix: Aim for eye level and finish with your thumbs pointing back.
Keep shoulders down and squeeze your upper back.

makeItEasier.name: Assisted Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Use assistance or a band to reduce body weight while keeping full range.

levelUp.description: Add a 2-second squeeze at the top of each rep to build upper-back control.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you release the rope, breathe out as you pull your hands to your face.

name: Deadlift
description: Total-body strength with a hip hinge.

setup: Start from the floor — position the bar over mid-foot and hinge down to grip the bar just outside your legs.
Keep your back flat, chest up, and shoulders just over or slightly in front of the bar.
Brace your core and set your lats; you're ready to drive through the floor to stand.
movement: First phase — push the floor away with your legs and drive the bar up; keep the bar close to your body.
As the bar passes your knees, extend your hips to stand tall—second phase is hip extension.
Lower by hinging and bending the knees, bar staying close; set it down with control or go straight into the next rep.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel strong tension in your hamstrings, with your lats, core supporting a stable spine.

commonFixes.fix: Hinge at the hips and keep your spine long. Lower only as far as you can hold that shape.
Keep the bar close to your legs and lightly graze your shins and thighs as you move.

makeItEasier.name: Hip Hinge with Dowel
makeItEasier.description: Use a light stick to learn the hinge without load.

levelUp.description: Increase load gradually and add a 1-second squeeze at full hip extension.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in before you lift, brace, then breathe out as you stand tall with the bar.

name: Barbell Overhead Press
description: Builds strong, stable shoulders.

setup: Stand with feet about hip width, core braced and glutes lightly squeezed.
Take the bar at collarbone height with a grip just outside shoulder width.
Keep your wrists straight and elbows slightly in front of the bar.
movement: Press the bar straight up overhead—not toward your chest.
