instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in on the way down to the hang, breathe out as you pull up.

makeItEasier.name: Pull-Up
makeItEasier.description: Achieve 8-10 clean bodyweight pull-ups before adding load.

levelUp.description: Progressively increase load using a dipping belt. Track added weight as you would a barbell exercise.

name: Band Pull-Apart
description: Horizontal shoulder health exercise for rear delts and upper back.
siblingReason: Horizontal shoulder health exercise. Directly counterbalances the internal rotation and anterior delt dominance that accumulates from pressing volume. Should be programmed alongside all pressing-heavy sessions. Not a rowing exercise — it trains the rear delt and external rotators specifically.

setup: Hold a resistance band at shoulder height with both hands, arms extended in front of you.
Use an overhand grip with hands about shoulder width apart.
Keep your shoulders down and away from your ears before you begin.
movement: Pull the band apart by driving both arms out to the sides, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the end.
Control the return — don't let the band snap back. Move slowly in both directions.
Keep your arms at shoulder height throughout — don't let them drop.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel the back of your shoulders and the muscles between your shoulder blades working. Your chest will naturally open as you pull apart.

commonFixes.fix: Think about pulling your shoulders down before each rep. The movement is shoulder blades together, not shoulders up.
Keep eyes on a fixed point straight ahead and maintain arms at shoulder height throughout the movement.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe out as you pull the band apart and squeeze. Breathe in as you return.

makeItEasier.name: Face Pull
makeItEasier.description: Face pull provides similar shoulder health benefit with more movement guidance.

levelUp.description: Use a stiffer band or add reps. Can also perform with slight external rotation (thumbs up) for greater rotator cuff emphasis.

name: Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch
description: Stretch for hip flexors and psoas in a half-kneeling position.

setup: Kneel on one knee with the other foot flat on the floor in front, knee at 90 degrees.
Keep your torso upright — don't lean forward.
Tuck your pelvis slightly under to increase the stretch in the hip flexor of the kneeling leg.
movement: Shift your weight gently forward until you feel a stretch at the front of the hip of the kneeling leg.
Hold the position, breathing slowly and allowing the hip flexor to relax.
Deepen the stretch on each exhale — never force the range of motion.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a stretch at the front of the hip of the rear kneeling leg. If you feel it in your lower back, re-tuck your pelvis and reduce how far you shift forward.

commonFixes.issue: Feeling it in the lower back not the hip
commonFixes.fix: Tuck your pelvis under before shifting forward. Keep your core gently engaged throughout.
Keep your chest tall and shoulder blades back. The stretch comes from the hip shift, not from leaning.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in to set up, breathe out slowly and ease deeper into the stretch. Avoid holding your breath.

makeItEasier.name: Hamstring Stretch
makeItEasier.description: General lower body flexibility work.

levelUp.description: Add a gentle side lean away from the kneeling leg to increase the stretch into the TFL and lateral hip flexors.

name: Thoracic Rotation (Quadruped)
description: Mobility drill for thoracic spine and rotation.

setup: Start on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips.
Place one hand behind your head with the elbow pointing out to the side.
Keep your lower back neutral — don't let it arch or round.
movement: Rotate your elbow toward the opposite hand on the floor, letting your upper back round slightly.
Then rotate upward and open, driving the elbow toward the ceiling as far as your thoracic spine allows.
Follow the movement with your eyes — look down then look up. Move slowly and with control.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel the rotation happening through your upper and middle back. Your lower back and hips should stay relatively still — the movement is in the thoracic spine, not the lumbar.

commonFixes.fix: Think about keeping your hips square and still. Place a foam roller or stick across your lower back to feel if it moves — it should not.
This is common with thoracic stiffness. Consistency is more important than range. Gently explore the end range without forcing.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe out as you rotate upward and open. Breathe in as you return to the start.

makeItEasier.name: Shoulder Dislocations (Band)
makeItEasier.description: General upper body mobility work.

levelUp.description: Thread the needle stretch or open book stretch for greater thoracic rotation range.

name: Ankle Mobility Wall Drill
description: Drill to improve ankle dorsiflexion and mobility.

setup: Stand facing a wall, about one foot's length away.
Place one foot forward with toes touching the wall.
Keep your heel flat on the floor throughout the drill.
movement: Drive your knee forward toward the wall, keeping your heel on the floor.
Try to touch the wall with your knee without your heel lifting.
If your knee touches the wall easily, move your foot further back and repeat.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel a stretch at the front of the ankle and into the calf. Your heel must stay down — if it lifts, move your foot closer to the wall.

commonFixes.fix: Move your foot closer to the wall. The goal is heel-down contact, not maximum distance. Build the range over time.
Drive your knee directly over your second toe — the middle of your foot. Ankle mobility restricts the knee tracking outward.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe steadily throughout. Hold each rep for 2-3 seconds at end range.

makeItEasier.name: Bodyweight Squat
makeItEasier.description: Practice the squat pattern alongside ankle mobility work.

levelUp.description: Perform loaded with a light dumbbell held at chest. Weighted dorsiflexion increases the stretch stimulus.

name: Cable Bicep Curl
description: Bicep curl with constant cable tension through full range.
siblingReason: Constant cable tension maintains load through the full range of motion, including at the top of the curl where dumbbells become unloaded. Particularly effective for peak contraction and hypertrophy. Can be performed unilaterally or bilaterally.

setup: Set a low cable pulley and attach a straight bar or EZ bar handle.
Stand back from the machine, arms extended, elbows at your sides.
Keep your shoulders down and back before you begin.
movement: Curl the handle up by bending at the elbow, keeping your upper arms still and elbows at your sides.
Squeeze at the top and hold briefly before lowering with control.
Feel the cable maintain tension even at the top position — don't rush through this.
instructions.goodFormFeels: You should feel continuous bicep tension throughout the movement, including at full contraction at the top — unlike dumbbells which become unloaded at the top.

commonFixes.fix: Lower the weight and keep your core braced. The upper arms must stay fixed at your sides throughout.
Keep your elbows pointing toward the floor and pinned to your sides. Moving them forward reduces the bicep range of motion.

instructions.breathingCue: Breathe in as you lower, breathe out as you curl up.

makeItEasier.name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
makeItEasier.description: Build basic curl mechanics before using cable.

levelUp.description: Perform as a unilateral cable curl or use an incline cable setup for greater stretch at the bottom.
